skill.name: agent-development
skill.description: Guidance for creating Claude Code plugin agents with structured frontmatter, clear triggering examples, system prompts, tool limits, and validation checks.
skill.summary: Create reliable Claude Code plugin agents with strong prompts and trigger rules.
skill.author: anthropics

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no malicious behavior or prompt injection. Most detections are markdown code fences, placeholder ellipses, security-review examples, or read-only validation commands. The skill is publishable with low risk because it includes a local shell validator that reads a user-provided path.

low_findings.title: Read-Only Shell Validator Requires Normal Script Caution
low_findings.description: The skill includes a Bash validator that accepts an agent file path, verifies that it exists, and parses its contents with standard local tools. The path is quoted and the script does not write files, execute file contents, or perform network activity, so this is a low operational concern rather than malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script behavior is directly visible and limited to local validation. Confidence is high, but users should still review any shell script before running it.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Alerts Are Markdown and Benign Parsing
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many backticks in markdown files and command substitutions in the validator. The markdown backticks are formatting delimiters for examples, and the Bash substitutions read or parse a quoted local file path without untrusted command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged markdown regions are code fences, not executable Ruby or shell. The script uses fixed commands such as head, sed, awk, and grep with quoted variables.

low_findings.title: Sensitive, Crypto, And Traversal Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The sensitive-file and weak-cryptography alerts appear to be substring matches inside words, markdown paths, or security-review examples. The reviewed files do not contain credential theft, Windows SAM access, cryptographic implementation, or path traversal logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are parser variables or placeholder example text. No filesystem traversal, SAM database access, or weak cryptographic API usage was found in the reviewed context.

content.value_statement: Designing reliable autonomous agents is difficult without clear structure. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code users through agent frontmatter, triggering examples, prompts, and validation.
actual_capabilities: Explains the required markdown and YAML frontmatter structure for agents.
Shows how to write triggering descriptions with concrete example blocks.
Provides system prompt patterns for analysis, generation, validation, and orchestration agents.
Documents model, color, and tool access choices for Claude Code plugin agents.
Includes complete agent examples for code review, testing, documentation, and security analysis.
Provides a Bash validator for checking local agent markdown files.
limitations: It provides guidance and templates, not a running agent service.
Generated agents still need human review before use in a plugin.
The validation script checks structure but cannot prove agent behavior quality.
Examples target Claude Code plugin conventions and may need adaptation elsewhere.

use_cases.title: Build Plugin Agents
use_cases.description: Create well-formed agents for Claude Code plugins with names, descriptions, models, colors, tools, and system prompts.

use_cases.title: Standardize Agent Prompts
use_cases.description: Use repeatable prompt structures for review, testing, documentation, validation, and orchestration agents.

use_cases.title: Validate Agent Files
use_cases.description: Check local agent markdown files for required fields, frontmatter format, description quality, and prompt length.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one new agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code agent for [task]. Include the agent name, description, model, color, tool access, and system prompt.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Triggering Rules
prompt_templates.scenario: Refine when an agent should be used.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this agent description and improve the triggering examples. Make the examples specific, varied, and clear about when the agent should run.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Specialized Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Create an agent for a defined engineering workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a [review, testing, documentation, validation, or orchestration] agent for [project context]. Define responsibilities, process steps, quality standards, output format, and edge cases.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Agent Suite
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of multiple plugin agents.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit these Claude Code agent files for naming, trigger overlap, tool access, system prompt quality, and validation gaps. Recommend focused changes only.

output_examples.input: Create a code review agent for a TypeScript project.
output_examples.output: A complete agent plan with a clear identifier, review-focused trigger examples, read-only tool access, and a structured review prompt.

output_examples.input: Improve my test generator agent description.
output_examples.output: Rewritten trigger guidance that covers explicit test requests, proactive use after code changes, and clear commentary for each example.

output_examples.input: Check whether my agent file is valid.
output: A list of missing fields or weak descriptions.
Concrete fixes for frontmatter and prompt structure.

best_practices: Use specific trigger examples that reflect real user requests and project context.
Limit each agent to the tools it needs for its intended workflow.
Define responsibilities, process steps, output format, and edge cases in every system prompt.
anti_patterns: Do not use generic agent names or vague descriptions such as helper or assistant.
Do not grant full tool access when read-only access is enough.
Do not show the agent answering directly in trigger examples instead of being invoked.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create Claude Code plugin agents with valid frontmatter, trigger examples, system prompts, and tool settings.

faq.question: Does it run the agents for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides templates, examples, and validation guidance for agents used in Claude Code plugins.

faq.question: Can I use it with Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. Codex can use the guidance to draft or review Claude Code agent files in a shared workspace.

faq.question: What files does the validator change?
faq.answer: The validator reads the agent file that you pass to it. It does not write files or modify plugin content.

faq.question: How should I choose tools for an agent?
faq.answer: Start with the smallest tool set needed. Use read-only tools for analysis agents and add write or shell tools only when required.

faq.question: Why are trigger examples important?
faq.answer: Trigger examples teach Claude Code when to invoke the agent. Good examples reduce missed triggers and unwanted activations.
